text: In this video we will be describing laparoscopic myomectomy for a posterior cervical fibroid.
The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum in the triangle between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament to clip the uterine arteries, preparing for the laparoscopic myomectomy.
The uterine artery is visibly identified crossing from lateral to medial, with the ureter located approximately one centimeter caudal and medial to the artery.
The surgeon applies vascular clips to the uterine artery, securing it within the retroperitoneal space.
Two vascular clips are applied to the uterine artery, spaced approximately 0.5 centimeters apart, securing the vessel during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum on the opposite side and clips the uterine artery, mirroring the steps performed on the initial side.
The surgeon identifies the prominent vaginal artery as the second branch from the internal iliac and clips both the uterine and vaginal arteries to minimize bleeding during the myomectomy procedure.

After clipping the uterine and vaginal arteries, the surgeon makes an incision over the most prominent part of the fibroid located along the posterior cervix. The fibroid is then enucleated from the myoma bed, revealing the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects the myoma away from the myoma bed, with minimal bleeding visible due to the previously applied vascular clips on the uterine and vaginal arteries.

The surgeon performs lateralization of the ureter at the level of the posterior cervix to ensure safe suturing during the myomectomy procedure, highlighting the close proximity of the ureters to the surgical site.
The surgeon lateralizes the right ureter, moving it away from the surgical field towards the ureteric tunnel, to safely access the myoma located more towards the right side.
After fully mobilizing the ureter laterally to the level of the ureteric tunnel, the surgeon begins suturing the myoma bed using a quill suture.
The surgeon uses a three-dimensional laparoscope to enhance visualization while suturing deep within the surgical field.
and one needs to have extremely good skills with ergonomic approach towards suturing in such deep spaces.
The surgeon completes suturing in two layers to fully obliterate the myoma bed at the level of the posterior cervix, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
After completing the suturing, the surgeon uses a disposable morcellator blade to enucleate the remaining myoma from the peritoneal cavity.
